content.text: When to Use Feature Stores vs Vector Stores

Feature stores and vector stores solve different problems. Choosing the right one — or both — depends on what kind of context your LLM application needs at inference time. This guide gives concrete decision criteria, anti-patterns, and integration patterns for each combination.

attrs.alt: Decision flowchart: if you need document retrieval, use a vector store; if you need user/entity personalisation, use a feature store; if you need both, use both; if you need neither, a prompt-only design is sufficient.

content.text: Two independent questions drive the choice:
Do you need semantic / document retrieval?
Are you grounding the LLM in a corpus of documents, policies, FAQs, or code? If yes, you need a vector store.
Do you need user or entity personalisation?
Does the LLM response need to know the user's subscription tier, language, history summary, or other structured profile data? If yes, you need a feature store.
The two needs are orthogonal — you can have either, both, or neither.

If the LLM's system prompt is the same for every user and every query, you need neither store. Examples: a code formatter, a creative writing aid, a fixed-domain classifier. Adding infrastructure for retrieval or personalisation before you have evidence of their value adds latency, complexity, and cost with no return.
Start here. Add vector or feature stores only when a measurable quality gap — factual errors, irrelevant responses, wrong user experience — points to missing context.
All users get the same knowledge base (company FAQ, product docs, legal corpus).
The relevant context is large enough that it cannot fit in a single prompt — you must retrieve a subset per query.
Answers must cite or ground on specific source documents.
The system serves anonymous or unauthenticated users who have no persistent profile.
Signals that you need to add a feature store later
Users complain the assistant does not know who they are or repeats already-answered questions.
You are manually injecting user attributes (language, plan) by string interpolation in code — this is the feature store's job.
Different services need the same user attributes, leading to duplicated DB queries with divergent logic.
The LLM task is personalised but self-contained — no external knowledge corpus required.

Examples: a writing-style assistant that adapts tone to user preference, a customer-service bot that greets users by name and references their plan, a notification-copy generator that adapts reading level per user.
Context is structured (scalars, short strings) — not unstructured documents.
Sub-5ms latency for context lookup is required; vector search at 20–50ms would be too slow.
Signals that you need to add a vector store later
LLM regularly hallucinates product details, policy clauses, or factual answers that exist in internal documents.
Users ask questions whose answers require a corpus too large to fit in the system prompt.
You maintain a large system prompt that copies text from a knowledge base — the vector store should serve that.

When users need both retrieval (domain knowledge) and personalisation (user context), combine the two. The feature store supplies per-user structured context injected into the system prompt; the vector store supplies the top-K relevant chunks for the current query. Both arrive in the same LLM call.

Running the two lookups in parallel matters: feature store latency is ~2ms (Redis), vector store latency is ~20ms (ANN). Parallel execution means wall-clock is ~20ms total, not ~22ms — a small saving per request but significant at high throughput.
Relevant case law and statute chunks
Using a Vector Store for Structured Lookups

Embedding a user's subscription tier into a vector store and doing a similarity search to retrieve it is wrong. Structured attributes are exact — a user either has subscription_tier='premium' or they don't. ANN search introduces unnecessary latency (20ms vs < 2ms Redis) and risks returning the wrong entity's value.

# WRONG: structured lookup via vector search
results = vector_store.search(f"user {user_id} subscription tier", top_k=1)
tier = results[0].payload["tier"]   # fragile, slow, unnecessary

# RIGHT: exact lookup from feature store
tier = feature_store.read(user_id, ["subscription_tier"])["subscription_tier"]
Using a Feature Store for Document Retrieval

Storing thousands of document chunks in a Redis feature store and iterating over them to find the nearest one is O(n) at query time — fine for < 1 000 documents, unusable beyond that. A vector store with an ANN index is the right tool for corpus search.

A common trap: the LLM service queries the user DB, the billing service, and the history service directly on every request, then merges results inline. This is an ad-hoc feature store with no caching, no freshness guarantees, no monitoring, and duplicated logic across every service that needs the same data.

# ANTI-PATTERN: rebuild features on every request
def get_user_context(user_id: str) -> dict:
    user = db.query("SELECT language, tier FROM users WHERE id = ?", user_id)
    history = history_db.get_recent_turns(user_id, n=10)
    summary = summarise(history)   # expensive, no caching
    return {"language": user.language, "tier": user.tier, "summary": summary}
    # Every service that needs this context duplicates this function

# BETTER: pre-compute and serve from feature store
features = feature_store.read(user_id, ["language", "subscription_tier", "conversation_summary"])
Ships fast; adds infra only if gap is proven
No external infra; fits in memory
Redis hash per user (manual feature store)
One Redis instance; no framework overhead
Each store optimised for its own access pattern
Most teams follow this progression as their LLM application matures:
Validate the product idea. System prompt is static. No retrieval, no personalisation.
Corpus grows beyond what fits in context. Add chunking, embedding, and vector search for the knowledge base.
Inject user attributes via direct DB query. Acceptable while the feature set is small and used by one service.
More services need the same features. Freshness SLAs tighten. Cache misses cause latency spikes. Introduce Redis-backed feature caching.
Streaming features required, multiple teams writing features, training/serving consistency problems appear. Adopt Feast, Tecton, or a managed equivalent.

Resist jumping to Stage 4/5 from Stage 1. Each stage adds operational surface area — pipelines to monitor, caches to warm, schemas to version. The jump is only worth it when the previous stage shows concrete scaling or consistency problems.
Evaluating Readiness: Checklist Before Adopting Each Store
Answer these questions honestly before standing up vector infrastructure:
Is the total corpus content larger than your LLM's context window? If every document fits in a single prompt, vector search adds latency and complexity with no benefit.
Do users ask open-ended questions that map to different document subsets? If every user asks roughly the same thing, pre-compiling a static context block is simpler.
Have you chosen an embedding model you can commit to? Re-embedding a large corpus when you change models costs compute and downtime. Lock in the model before scaling the index.
Have you tested chunk size and overlap on representative queries? Chunk size affects retrieval quality more than ANN index choice for most corpora. Test before optimising the index.
Does your product have headroom for an additional 20–50ms ANN search per request? If you are already at latency limits, optimise other components first.
Answer these before building feature pipelines:
Is the same user attribute needed by more than one service or model? A feature store earns its cost when it eliminates duplicated computation across callers.

What is the maximum acceptable staleness for each feature? Write down a concrete SLA (e.g. subscription tier within 5 minutes, conversation summary within 10 seconds). If you cannot state it, the feature store's freshness guarantees will be untestable.

How many entities × features × updates per second will you generate? A single Redis instance handles millions of keys; you only need a feature framework when streaming computation or training/serving consistency is required.
Who owns the pipeline that keeps features fresh? A feature store with no owner degrades silently — feature values grow stale, alerts go unchecked, and the LLM quietly receives wrong context.
Will you use these features in model training as well as inference? If yes, point-in-time correctness (the offline store's join semantics) becomes non-negotiable.

When both stores are in production, monitor them as a combined context pipeline, not as two independent systems. An outage in either degrades the LLM's context and therefore its output quality, even if the LLM itself is healthy.
Cache cold, TTL too short, or entity miss
HNSW index fragmented or CPU starved
Both stores sit in the critical path to LLM responses. Design explicit fallbacks for each:

def retrieve_with_fallback(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[str]:
    """Return retrieved chunks, or an empty list if the store is unavailable."""
    try:
        chunks = vector_store.search(query, top_k=top_k, timeout_ms=100)
        return [c.text for c in chunks]
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.warning("vector_store_fallback query=%r err=%s", query[:50], exc)
        metrics.increment("vector_store.fallback")
        # Continue without retrieval context; LLM will answer from its own knowledge
        # or decline if its system prompt requires context
        return []

A degraded context pipeline is better than a 500 error. Use tight timeouts on both stores and return empty/default context on failure — the LLM can still respond, possibly with reduced quality, rather than the user seeing an error page.

Use a vector store when the application must retrieve relevant document chunks from a corpus that is too large for a single prompt — the classic RAG pattern. Use a feature store when structured per-user or per-entity context must be served at sub-5ms latency to multiple services without duplicated computation. Use both for personalised RAG: run the two lookups in parallel and merge into a single system prompt. Start without either, add infrastructure only when a measurable quality or freshness gap makes the cost worthwhile, and prefer simple solutions (ChromaDB in-process, Redis hash) before introducing framework complexity.
